canonical_name: Foreign Entity of Concern Interpretive Guidance
facts: In May 2024, the Department of Energy (DOE) issued its final interpretive guidance on the statutory definition of “foreign entity of concern” (FEOC) in the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act (IIJA).
The guidance is designed to reduce reliance on FEOCs in battery supply chains and bolster the growth of domestic and friend-shored battery materials and manufacturing.
The guidance is largely finalized as proposed in December 2023 with refinements and clarification to aid automakers and other stakeholders in identifying FEOCs in their battery supply chains.
The DOE final guidance applies to DOE’s Battery Materials Processing and Manufacturing grant program authorized by section 40207 of IIJA.
The program imposes limits when an entity’s battery supply chain includes foreign entities of concern.

A foreign entity is defined as a “foreign entity of concern” if it is “owned by, controlled by, or subject to the jurisdiction or direction of a government of a foreign country that is a covered nation.”
Covered nations are defined as Peoples Republic of China, Russia, Iran, and North Korea.

The final interpretive guidance clarifies the definition of “foreign entity of concern” by providing interpretations of four key terms: “government of a foreign country,” “foreign entity,” “subject to the jurisdiction,” and “owned by, controlled by, or subject to the direction.”

DOE’s interpretive guidance provides clarity on how to identify entities that are FEOCs as a result of being owned by, controlled by, or subject to the jurisdiction or direction of the government of a covered nation.
An entity incorporated in, headquartered in, or performing relevant activities in a covered nation is subject to the jurisdiction of a covered nation and is therefore a FEOC.

A company with at least 25 percent voting rights, board seats, or equity interests held by the government of a covered nation (including a senior official of such a government or dominant political party) is a FEOC, regardless of where those activities occur.

DOE’s guidance establishes a set of rights that an entity may reserve within contractual agreements to eliminate the potential for effective control if that entity has entered into a license agreement or contract with a FEOC.
There is no consolidated list of all entities considered FEOCs under section 40207(a)(5) of IIJA.

The statute identifies certain entities as FEOCs if they appear on lists maintained by other agencies, such as entities designated as foreign terrorist organizations by the Secretary of State and individuals/entities on the SDN List maintained by OFAC.
Individuals alleged by the Attorney General to have been involved in illegal activities like espionage and arms exports, for which a conviction was obtained, are also identified as FEOCs.
The term “covered nation” is defined in 10 U.S.C. 4872(f)(2) to mean North Korea, the People’s Republic of China (PRC), Russia, and Iran.

DOE interprets “foreign entity” to include a government of a foreign country, a natural person who is not a lawful permanent resident or citizen of the US, a partnership/corporation organized under foreign laws or with principal business in a foreign country, or a US entity owned/controlled by such an entity.
The 25 percent ownership threshold was set because it is a widely used standard to identify control by a parent company in the private sector.
Regulatory contexts using the 25 percent threshold include the Department of Commerce’s CHIPS rules, the Department of State’s ITAR, and the Treasury Department’s BSA private banking regulation.

A U.S. subsidiary of a foreign company is generally only considered a FEOC if it is headquartered/incorporated in a covered nation or at least 25 percent of its voting interest, board seats, or equity interests are held by a government of a covered nation.

The policy objective behind the guidance is to understand and mitigate the influence of a covered nation's government in the battery and critical mineral supply chain by assessing each entity’s relationship to that government.
DOE intentionally included specific reference to the National People’s Congress as an example of an entity whose current and former members qualify as “senior foreign political figures.”
To determine if an official is “senior,” they must be or have been in a position of substantial authority over policy, operations, or the use of government-owned resources.

Current and former members of the National People’s Congress satisfy the substantial authority standard and qualify as senior foreign political figures due to its transparent list of members serving five-year terms.
The final guidance omitted specific reference to the National Party Congress but anticipates participants will likely meet the substantial authority standard.
DOE's FEOC guidance is very similar to the CHIPS rule, with intentional distinctions driven by differences between battery and semiconductor markets and IIJA vs. CHIPS and Science Act.
DOE and the Department of Commerce coordinated throughout development and largely adopted the same interpretation of jurisdiction, ownership, and control.

DOE’s guidance considers an entity a FEOC under three scenarios: via jurisdiction of a covered nation's government, via 25% ownership/control by a covered nation's government, and via certain licenses or contracts with a FEOC.
The third provision addressing effective control through license/contract agreements is not found in the CHIPS rule and addresses means of control specific to the battery supply chain.
DOE’s guidance presents a strong signal that the U.S. does not see suppliers based in the PRC as part of a reliable or secure energy supply chain.
PRC-headquartered companies, PRC state-owned enterprises, and companies controlled by the PRC government are treated as FEOCs.

This includes any company producing an applicable mineral or battery component in China, as well as companies subject to 25 percent PRC government control through voting rights, board seats, or equity interests.

Individuals holding or having held senior positions within the PRC government or dominant ruling party (Chinese Communist Party), and their family members, are considered extensions of government control.
An entity not based in China or directly/indirectly controlled by the PRC government may still be a FEOC based on effective control through licensing agreements with another FEOC.

DOE will continue to closely monitor the market for PRC government influence and may incorporate non-ownership measures like direct/indirect investments to inform potential designation of entities as FEOCs under IIJA Section 40207(a)(5)(E).
source_title: Foreign Entity of Concern Interpretive Guidance | Department of Energy
